purpose: Explains what sustains corporate profits and by extension the economic expansion. Answers: why hasn't the expansion ended despite deteriorating leading indicators?
components: Profits = Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports − Household Saving + Dividends
Government deficit of 6-7% GDP as the dominant profit source
Sectoral balance identity as the macro accounting constraint
Mundell-Fleming open economy extensions for exchange rate and capital flow implications
current_assessment: The fiscal deficit is the load-bearing variable sustaining corporate profits at 1.5-2.4pp GDP contribution. Private-sector-generated profits are only sufficient for 1.0-1.5% growth. All 8 agents converge on this framework, which is itself flagged as a risk signal — unanimity on an unfalsifiable framework may indicate analytical monoculture.
limitations: Framework unanimity across all agents is itself a risk signal
Functionally unfalsifiable — explains why indicators failed, why the cycle extended, why the private sector is healthy
Only one proposed falsification condition: economy grows >2.5% for 4 quarters while deficit narrows >1pp GDP
Ricardian equivalence partial offset (30-50%) is acknowledged by only one agent
Distributional adequacy of the surplus is unaddressed by the aggregate identity

purpose: Classifies financial structures by fragility to identify systemic credit risk accumulation. Answers: where is hidden leverage and what triggers the transition from stability to instability?
components: Hedge finance: income covers principal and interest (hyperscaler AI capex)
Speculative finance: income covers interest but not principal (data center REITs at 5-7x)
Ponzi finance: income covers neither — requires asset price appreciation (GPU leasing, AI venture debt)
Endogenous transition mechanism: stability breeds complacency breeds fragility
current_assessment: The credit ecosystem shows bifurcation: hedge-financed mega-cap core vs. increasingly speculative/Ponzi downstream. EBITDA addbacks mask the true Minsky classification by overstating income coverage. Covenant-lite structures delay the recognition of Ponzi transitions. The maturity wall (2025-2027) is the likely forcing function for reclassification.
limitations: Downstream AI Ponzi quantification is too vague to be actionable (self-assessed 5/10)
Without bottom-up aggregation, aggregate systemic interconnection is unknown
The scale of the current covenant-lite cycle is unprecedented, making step-function predictions untested at this scale
Private credit opacity prevents accurate real-time Minsky classification

purpose: Provides historical base rates for how long general-purpose technologies take to produce aggregate productivity gains. Answers: when will AI produce measurable TFP improvement?
components: Electricity: ~30 years from invention to aggregate productivity breakthrough
IT/computing: ~15 years from widespread adoption to productivity acceleration
Multiplicative transmission chain: micro gains × adoption rate × reallocation efficiency × complementary investment
Each multiplicative term currently well below 1.0 for AI
current_assessment: AI firm-level gains are real (14% customer support, 30-40% writing tasks) but aggregate nonfarm TFP remains at ~1.5-2.0% with no structural break. Base rates suggest 5-15 years for material aggregate impact. The contrarian argument (AI is recursive and deploys over existing digital infrastructure) self-assessed at 4/10 confidence.
limitations: Historical analogues may not apply to a recursive technology deploying over existing infrastructure
U.S.-China technology decoupling could extend diffusion beyond base rates
Sample size is inherently small (only a few GPTs in history)
Neither the acceleration nor deceleration argument is currently falsifiable

purpose: Identifies the inconsistency between equity-implied and rates-implied nominal GDP growth to determine which market is mispriced. Answers: are equities or rates correct about the growth outlook?
components: Equity-implied NGDP: S&P at ~21x fwd P/E with ~10% EPS growth implies 5-6%
Rates-implied NGDP: forwards embedding 75-100bp easing imply 3-4%
HY OAS at 350-420bp as a tiebreaker (leans equity narrative)
Causal interpretation: equities price fiscal-sustained economy, rates price private-sector-only economy
Historical base rate: ~60-65% rates correct, but imprecise post-COVID
current_assessment: The divergence is real and unsustainable. The macro_strategist_02 interpretation (equities and rates are each correct about different economies — the fiscal-sustained vs. private-sector-only) won debate as the cleanest causal explanation. Resolution determines whether HY spreads are fairly valued or 100-200bp too tight.
limitations: ~60-65% historical base rate favoring rates is insufficiently sourced and may be impaired post-COVID
Direction of resolution remains low confidence (6/10)
HY OAS as tiebreaker may itself be distorted by private credit and covenant-lite dynamics

purpose: Provides an explicit historical sequence for how the current fiscal-driven mid-cycle slowdown could evolve, with a resolution window. Answers: what happens next and when?
components: Phase 1: Indicator deterioration without recession (yield curve, LEI, Sahm Rule)
Phase 2: Credit mini-crisis
Phase 3: Fed policy reversal
Phase 4: Re-acceleration
Phase 5: Inflation acceleration
Phase 6: Eventual recession (3 years after initial scare — Dec 1969 in analogue, implying 2027-2028 current)
Five specific signposts with frequencies and thresholds for real-time tracking
current_assessment: Highest-ranked historical analogue despite imperfect fit. Current deficit (6-7%) is 2x the 1966-67 level (3-4%). No prior analogue captures the maturity wall, covenant-lite structures, or private credit opacity simultaneously. The analogue suggests the current cycle resolves into inflation acceleration and eventual recession in 2027-2028 if the pattern holds.
limitations: All historical analogues are inherently imperfect
Current deficit is 2x the 1966-67 level
No analogue captures maturity wall, covenant-lite, and private credit opacity
Similarity scores (0.72) refuted as false precision — ranking defensible, scores not
1946-48 analogue has more structural parallels but less operational relevance

purpose: Quantifies the cost of last-mile disinflation in terms of output loss. Answers: how much growth must be sacrificed to bring core PCE from ~2.5-3% to the 2% target?
components: NKPC convexity near potential output
Sacrifice ratio of 4-5x for last-mile disinflation
Fiscal floor preventing sufficient output gap opening
Sargent-Wallace fiscal dominance as the macro constraint
Supply-chain normalization gains exhausted — remaining inflation is demand-driven
current_assessment: Core PCE has stalled at ~2.5-3% after rapid decline from 5.6%. The fiscal deficit prevents the output gap from opening enough for the 4-5x sacrifice ratio to operate. This creates a policy trap where the Fed cannot achieve its inflation target without fiscal cooperation that is politically unavailable. 5Y5Y breakevens at 2.3% suggest partial de-anchoring.
limitations: Sacrifice ratio estimate is imprecise and regime-dependent
A favorable supply shock could provide exit without demand destruction
Orphanides/van Norden output gap measurement error of ±1.0-2.0pp makes real-time assessment difficult

purpose: Provides the accounting constraint linking government, private, and external sector financial positions. Answers: what is the mechanical consequence of the fiscal deficit for private sector balance sheets?
components: Government deficit = Private surplus + Current account deficit
Verifiable in Fed Z.1 Financial Accounts
Comparison with prior cycles: 1999-2000 (-5% private deficit), 2005-2007 (-3-4% private deficit)
current_assessment: The identity confirms the private sector surplus exists as a direct fiscal artifact. The surplus is the strongest argument against a near-term 2008-style outcome, but its distributional adequacy is the most important unanswered question. If concentrated in entities that don't interact with leveraged borrowers, the buffer narrative collapses.
limitations: Aggregate identity masks distributional concerns
Cannot determine causation — only accounting relationships
Does not reveal which parts of the private sector hold the surplus
